review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about gradient descent, learning-rate choice, SGD versus full-batch updates, and why random initialization matters for word vectors. The concepts are stated directly, so no separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: Why must the learning rate in gradient descent usually be kept small?
choices: A large step always finds the global minimum faster
A large step can overshoot and even move to a worse point
A small step removes the need to compute gradients
A small step guarantees zero training error in one update
question_bank.explanation: The learning rate scales the update. If it is too large, the optimization step can jump past a good region and increase the objective instead of decreasing it.

question_bank.question: What is the main reason full-batch gradient descent is usually impractical for neural networks?
choices: It requires evaluating the objective and gradient on the whole training set before each update
It only works for convex losses
It prevents any randomness in initialization
question_bank.explanation: Full-batch gradient descent waits for a gradient over the entire training set before making one update, which is expensive for large datasets and large models.

transcript_quotes: problem for the basic gradient descent

question_bank.question: Why is initializing all word vectors to zero a bad idea?
choices: Zero vectors make the loss undefined
Zero vectors create symmetry so words receive the same updates
Zero vectors force the model to use full-batch training
Zero vectors make the vocabulary too large
question_bank.explanation: If every vector starts identically, the model cannot break symmetry: many words behave the same way and receive the same gradients, so useful distinctions never emerge.
